item_a.name: Greenworks 40V Cordless Lawn Mower
item_a.image_alt: Greenworks 40V cordless electric lawn mower with grass collection bag on lawn
pros: Lightweight design at 37 lbs makes maneuvering easy
Dual battery port allows extended runtime up to 70 minutes
Quieter operation at 75 dB compared to gas mowers
cons: Smaller 16-inch or 20-inch cutting deck options limit efficiency on larger lawns
Battery and charger often sold separately increases initial cost
Fewer attachment and accessory options in ecosystem
item_a.best_for: Homeowners with small to medium yards who prioritize lightweight design and battery compatibility across multiple tools.

item_b.name: Ryobi 40V Brushless Lawn Mower
item_b.image_alt: Ryobi 40V brushless cordless lawn mower with self-propelled feature on residential lawn
pros: Larger 21-inch cutting deck covers more ground per pass
Extensive ONE+ battery system compatible with 300+ tools
Self-propelled models available for easier operation on slopes
Brushless motor provides 20% more runtime and power
cons: Heavier at 48-58 lbs depending on model
3-year tool warranty is shorter than Greenworks
Battery charging time can reach 90-120 minutes for larger capacity batteries
item_b.best_for: DIY enthusiasts with medium to large yards who want compatibility with a vast tool ecosystem and self-propelled convenience.

comparison_summary: This matchup really depends on your lawn size and how deep you want to go with cordless tools. Greenworks is lighter with dual battery ports and a 5-year warranty, while Ryobi brings a larger cutting deck, brushless motor tech, and compatibility with over 300 tools in the ONE+ system.
verdict_text: Ryobi takes this for most homeowners with its 21-inch deck, brushless efficiency, and 300+ tool compatibility, but Greenworks wins if you value lightweight handling and better warranty coverage for smaller yards.
seo_content:
I've spent years testing battery-powered lawn equipment, and the Greenworks vs Ryobi debate comes up constantly. Both brands have essentially redefined cordless mowing, but they've taken completely different paths to get there.

Let's start with the cutting deck. Ryobi sticks with the traditional 21-inch width you'd find on gas mowers, while Greenworks typically uses 20-inch decks. That extra inch means you're covering about 25% more ground with each pass on the Ryobi. For lawns over 1/4 acre, that difference actually matters—you'll finish noticeably faster.

But here's where Greenworks fights back: weight. Their mowers clock in around 37-40 pounds compared to Ryobi's hefty 48-58 pound range. That lighter design makes a real difference if you're maneuvering around flower beds, hauling the mower up basement stairs, or dealing with uneven ground. I genuinely appreciate this on smaller yards where you're constantly changing direction.

The battery situation is fascinating. Greenworks went with dual battery ports on many models, letting you run two 4.0Ah batteries simultaneously for up to 70 minutes of runtime. It's clever—you get extended runtime without buying expensive high-capacity batteries. Ryobi takes a different approach with single larger batteries paired with brushless motors that squeeze out about 20% more efficiency than brushed designs. Those brushless motors also last longer and need less maintenance over time.

From an ecosystem standpoint, Ryobi's ONE+ platform is massive—over 300 compatible tools including lawn equipment, power tools, cleaning devices, even portable electronics. That's serious versatility. Greenworks supports 60+ tools, which is still plenty but focuses more on outdoor equipment specifically.

Warranty coverage favors Greenworks with 5 years on tools versus Ryobi's 3 years. Battery warranties are comparable at 2-3 years for both. Pricing generally puts Greenworks $50-100 lower in similar categories, though kit configurations vary widely. Self-propelled options appear more consistently across Ryobi's lineup, which matters if you have slopes or larger properties.

For yards under 1/4 acre where maneuverability counts most, I'd lean toward Greenworks. The lighter weight and better warranty make sense here. But for bigger properties or if you're building out a full cordless tool collection, Ryobi's brushless efficiency, larger deck, and ONE+ compatibility justify the extra cost and weight.

faq.q: Are Greenworks and Ryobi batteries interchangeable?
faq.a: No, they're completely incompatible. Each brand uses its own proprietary battery design, so you can't mix and match. You're basically choosing an ecosystem, not just a mower.

faq.q: Which brand offers better runtime on a single charge?
faq.a: Greenworks gives you up to 70 minutes if you use the dual battery setup, while Ryobi delivers 40-50 minutes with a single 5.0Ah battery. The difference is that Ryobi's brushless motors use power more efficiently, so you get more consistent cutting performance as the battery drains.

faq.q: Do Greenworks or Ryobi mowers work well for thick or tall grass?
faq.a: Ryobi handles overgrown grass better thanks to the extra torque from its brushless motors. Greenworks does fine in normal conditions, but you'll probably need to slow down or make extra passes if you let the grass get really thick or tall.

faq.q: Which brand has better availability for replacement parts and service?
faq.a: Ryobi has the edge here since it's sold exclusively through Home Depot, making it easy to get help or parts in person. Greenworks sells through multiple retailers but you might need to order specialized parts online. Their longer warranty helps offset that inconvenience though.

faq.q: Can these mowers handle hills and slopes effectively?
faq.a: Ryobi's self-propelled models are better on slopes because of their variable speed drive and extra weight for traction. Greenworks is easier to push uphill since it's lighter, but it doesn't have the power-assisted control that really reduces fatigue on challenging terrain.

faq.q: Is Greenworks 40V Cordless Lawn Mower better than Ryobi 40V Brushless Lawn Mower?
faq.a: It depends on your priorities. Ryobi wins overall for most homeowners thanks to its larger 21-inch deck, brushless motor efficiency, and massive 300+ tool ecosystem, but Greenworks edges ahead if you have a smaller yard, value lightweight design, and prefer the superior 5-year warranty coverage.

faq.q: Should I buy Greenworks 40V Cordless Lawn Mower or Ryobi 40V Brushless Lawn Mower?
faq.a: Choose Ryobi if you want the best all-around performance, faster cutting with a bigger deck, and plan to build out a cordless tool collection. Pick Greenworks if you prioritize ease of handling, have a smaller lawn, and want maximum warranty protection and dual battery flexibility.

faq.q: What is the difference between Greenworks 40V Cordless Lawn Mower and Ryobi 40V Brushless Lawn Mower?
faq.a: Ryobi features a 21-inch cutting deck and brushless motor for superior efficiency, plus compatibility with 300+ ONE+ tools, while Greenworks is lighter with dual battery ports and a 5-year warranty. Ryobi is built for larger properties and expanding tool collections, whereas Greenworks suits smaller yards and users who prioritize weight and warranty.
